title-group.article-title:
Novel approach to a fast calculation of high-order
Q
cumulants
title-group.alt-title: NOVEL APPROACH TO A FAST CALCULATION OF …
NAĐĐERĐ, MILOŠEVIĆ, DEVETAK, WANG, AND ZHU

contrib-group.aff:
1
https://ror.org/04wecwh56 Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences —National Institute of the Republic of Serbia, https://ror.org/02qsmb048 University of Belgrade , Mike Petrovića Alasa 12–14, Vinča 11351, Belgrade, Serbia
2 Strong Coupling Physics International Research Laboratory, https://ror.org/04mvpxy20 Huzhou University , Huzhou, Zhejiang 313000, People's Republic of China
3 Department of Physics and Astronomy, https://ror.org/02dqehb95 Purdue University , Indiana 47907, USA

permissions.copyright-statement: Published by the American Physical Society

license.license-p: Published by the American Physical Society under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license. Further distribution of this work must maintain attribution to the author(s) and the published article's title, journal citation, and DOI. Funded by SCOAP3.

abstract.p:
The method of
Q
cumulants has been shown as a powerful tool to study the fine details of the azimuthal anisotropies in high-energy nucleus-nucleus collisions. A new method for the fast calculation of arbitrary order
Q
-cumulant
v n
{ 2 k }
values, based on the partition of a non-negative integer
l ≤ m
for calculation of the
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations is presented in this paper. Unlike the standard
Q
-cumulants method in which the calculation of high-order multiparticle calculations is impractical, the newly proposed method enables easy calculation. The validity of the method is proven via a toy model that uses the elliptic power distribution to simulate anisotropic emission of particles. The method enables the study of fine details of the
v 2
distribution, such as higher-order central moments of the
v 2
distribution, as well as the hydrodynamic behavior of the quark-gluon plasma.

institution-wrap.institution: Ministry of Science, Technological Development and Innovation of Republic of Serbia
National Natural Science Foundation of China

sec.label: I.
sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p:
The quark-gluon plasma (QGP), a system consisting of strongly coupled quarks and gluons, is created in ultrarelativistic nuclear collisions [1–6]. The almondlike shape of the overlapping region in a nucleus-nucleus collision characterizes the initial spatial anisotropy. Due to this spatial anisotropy an anisotropic pressure gradient is built causing a collective azimuthally anisotropic expansion of the formed system. This phenomenon, called anisotropic collective flow, has been observed at the Brookhaven National Laboratory Alternating Gradient Synchrotron (E877) [7], the CERN Super Proton Synchrotron (NA49, WA98, CERES) [8–10], and the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider and Large Hadron Collider [6,11–13]. The anisotropic collective flow is quantified by Fourier harmonics
v n
[14] and is dominated by second-order elliptic flow because of the almond-shape overlap geometry [15]. The fluctuating positions of the nucleons in the overlapping region cause the appearance of the higher-order Fourier harmonics
v n
(
n > 2
)
. The most pronounced higher-order Fourier harmonic is the triangular flow (
v 3
).

A few methods have been developed to measure magnitudes of the Fourier harmonics [15–17]. The main challenge is to suppress short-range correlations arising from jets and resonance decays, commonly referred to as nonflow effects, to reveal genuine collective flow of the QGP. The idea of using cumulants to perform measurements of azimuthal anisotropies was first proposed in Refs. [18,19]. The method is based on cumulant expansion of multiparticle azimuthal correlations and uses the formalism of generating functions. The method mitigates contributions from nonflow effects by separating analyzed particles in pseudorapidity. A significant improvement of the method, called the
Q
-cumulants method [20], in principle, allows a fast calculation of all multiparticle correlations. Using this method, one can extract central moments of the
v n
distribution, for example skewness and kurtosis [21–23], to study hydrodynamic behavior of the QGP. The extraction of even higher-order central moments of the flow distribution requires higher-order
Q
cumulants, which makes their measurement worthwhile. In practice, however, a further increase of the order of
Q
cumulants, following the formalism given in Ref. [20], becomes difficult. Therefore, we developed a new approach for calculation of high-order
Q
cumulants.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section II describes basic variables of the
Q
-cumulant method. Section III presents foundation of the method. Section IV demonstrates the validity of the method. A summary is given in Sec. 5. A ROOT [24] code for calculating high-order
Q
cumulants is given in the Appendix.

sec.label: II.
sec.title:
BASICS OF THE
Q
CUMULANTS
sec.p:
The starting observable is flow vector
Q
defined as: (1)
Q n
=
∑
j = 1
M
e
i n
φ j
,
where
n
is the order of Fourier harmonics,
M
is the multiplicity (the number of particles detected in an event), and
φ j
is the azimuthal angle of the
j th
particle in the laboratory coordinate system. Within the method, the
2 m th
power of the magnitude of the flow vector,
|
Q n
|
2 m
: (2)
Q n
2 m
=
∑
j 1
, ... ,
j
2 m
= 1
M
e
i n
φ
j 1
+ ... +
φ
j m
−
φ
j
m + 1
− ... −
φ
j
2 m
,
is decomposed into off diagonal terms with
2 m
,
2 m
-1, … different indices up to the diagonal term with
2 m
equal indices. The first term of the decomposition, with
2 m
different indices, is proportional to the
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations denoted as
〈
2 m
〉
: (3)
∑
j 1
≠ ... ≠
j
2 m
= 1
M
e
i n
φ
j 1
+ ... +
φ
j m
−
φ
j
m + 1
− ... −
φ
j
2 m
≡
P
M , 2 m
·
2 m
,
where
P
M , 2 m
is the number of
2 m
-particle distinct combinations in an event with multiplicity
M
: (4)
P
M , 2 m
=
M !
( M − 2 m ) !
.

In the case of full decomposition,
|
Q n
|
2 m
is expressed in readily calculable terms of powers of the flow vector given with Eq. (1) along with the anticipated
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations introduced in Eq. (3). A thorough derivation with detailed examples is presented in Ref. [25]. The analytical decomposition of
|
Q n
|
2 m
for
m > 4
, however, becomes tedious.

In this paper we present a numerical method of decomposition of
|
Q n
|
2 m
that, with the use of modern computers, enables one to easily obtain analytical expressions of multiparticle azimuthal correlations of higher orders.

sec.label: III.
sec.title: FOUNDATION OF THE ALGORITHM
sec.p:
In our previous work [26] we have shown that the
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations
〈
2 m
〉
might be presented as a linear combination of basis vectors of a finite-dimensional vector space. In a comprehensive form this reads as: (5)
P
M , 2 m
2 m
=
∑
l = 0
m
f
( m , l )
∑
i = 1
δ l
N i
( m , l )
e i
( l )
,
where
N i
( m , l )
are integer numbers,
e i
( l )
are basis vectors of the corresponding
δ l
-dimensional subspace
W
δ l
.
f
( m , l )
are integer functions of multiplicity
M
given by (6)
f
( m , l )
=
( M − 2 l )
∏
j = m + l + 1
2 m − 1
( M − j )
,
for
l =
{ 0 , . . . , m − 2 }
M − 2 l ,
for
l = m − 1
1 ,
for
l = m
.

Basis vectors
e i
( l )
consist of an appropriate products of flow vectors,
Q
a 1
n
Q
a 2
n
Q
a 3
n
...
, with subscripts
a 1
n
,
a 2
n
,
a 3
n , ...
, which correspond to the partition of a non-negative integer
l =
a 1
+
a 2
+
a 3
+ ⋯
. Here we employed a convenient symbolic notation introduced in Refs. [20,25]: (7)
Q
a · n
≡
∑
j = 1
M
e
a · i n
φ j
, a ∈
{ 1 ,
2 ,
3 ,
... }
.

A partition of a non-negative integer
l
consists of
ν
distinct parts
b j
, each having the corresponding multiplicity
μ j
. For example, one of the partitions of number 12 is
3 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1
. It has ν = 3 distinct parts
b 1
= 3
,
b 2
= 2
, and
b 3
= 1
, each having the corresponding multiplicities
μ 1
= 2
,
μ 2
= 1
, and
μ 3
= 4
. In the multiplicity notation this might be written as
3
2
2
1
1
4
. Therefore, a composition of flow vectors consists of
ν
distinct subcompositions, where each sub-composition is a product of
μ j
number of flow vectors each having the subscript proportional to the same part
b j
: (8)
Q
b 1
n
Q
b 1
n
Q
b 1
n
⋯
︸
μ 1
Q
b 2
n
Q
b 2
n
⋯
︸
μ 2
⋯
⋯
Q
b ν
n
Q
b ν
n
⋯
︸
μ ν
=
∏
j = 1
ν
Q
b j
n
μ j
.

There are
p ( l )
distinct ways of representing a non-negative integer
l
as a sum of non-negative integers (partition function of a non-negative integer
l = {
0 ,
1 ,
2 ,
3 ,
4 ,
5
... .
}
is
p ( l ) = {
1 ,
1 ,
2 ,
3 ,
5 ,
7
...
}
). To obtain all basis vectors
e i
( l )
one needs to combine each composition of the flow vectors by the complex conjugates of each composition which gives in total
p ( l ) · p ( l )
supercompositions. Since a basis vector is represented only by the real value of a supercomposition, the real value of its complex-conjugate represents the same basis vector, and therefore one gets only
δ l
= p
( l )
[
p ( l ) + 1
]
/ 2
mutually independent basis vectors which span the whole subspace
W
δ l
. The total vector space of the
2 m
-particle correlations
V
d m
consists of
m + 1
subspaces,
V
d m
=
⊕
l = 0
m
W
δ l
, where
W
δ l
is spanned by basis vectors which contain supercompositions of flow vectors that correspond to the partition of only the integer
l
. The dimension of the total vector space is the sum of the dimensions of all subspaces
d m
=
∑
l = 0
m
δ l
. For example, vector space in the case of six-particle azimuthal correlations (
m = 3
),
V
m = 3
d
, consists of four subspaces
( l =
{
0 ,
1 ,
2 ,
3
}

( l )
=
{

}

δ l
=
{
1 ,

6
}
)
spanned by the following basis vectors
e i
( l )
(Table 3 in Ref. [26]): (9)
W
δ 0

( 0 )

)

≡ 2 Re
(
Q

2 n
*
)
,
e 6
( 3 )
≡ Re
(
Q
3 n
Q
3 n
*
)
,
with total dimension of
d 3
= 11
. In Eq. (9), we doubled the real values of the asymmetric supercompositions (those with nonequal
Q
and
Q
*
parts of the supercompositions) in order to simplify the formula for determining the integers
N i
( m , l )
.

In our previous work we obtained the integers
N i
( m , l )
by solving appropriate systems of linear algebraic equations [26]. By doing further investigations we revealed some interesting features of the Eq. (5) shown in Figs. 1 and 2, which guided us to obtain the explicit formula for determining the integers
N i
( m , l )
given by the Eq. (12) and Eq. (15). To illustrate this, we will write the first three well-known multiparticle azimuthal correlations [20]: (10)
P
M , 2

,

. The spline lines are to guide the eye.

Terms which contain partitions of the highest integer in each of these multiparticle correlations might be written in comprehensive form as follows: (11)
∑
i = 1
δ 1
N i
( 1 , 1 )

Q n

6

.

The integer coefficients
N i
( l , l )
are deducible from their known values for
l = 0 , ... , 7
,
i = 1 , ... ,
δ l
, [20,26], and are given by the following arithmetic function of the integer partition parameters
b
, μ, and
ν
, of the basis vectors: (12)
N i
( l , l )
=
( − 1 )
∑
j = 1
ν i
μ
i , j
l !
∏
j = 1
ν i
b
i , j
μ
i , j
·
μ
i , j
!
( − 1 )
∑
j = 1
ν ¯
i
μ ¯
i , j
l !
∏
j = 1
ν ¯
i
(
b ¯
i , j
μ ¯
i , j
·
μ ¯
i , j
!
)
,
where
ν i
counts the number of distinct parts of the integer partition in the
Q
part of the
i th
supercomposition (
i th
basis vector),
b
i , j
is the
j th
distinct part of the partition, and
μ
i , j
is its multiplicity.
ν ¯
i
,
b ¯
i , j
, and
μ ¯
i , j
are corresponding numbers in the
Q
*
part of the same supercomposition
i
.

For example, the integer coefficient
N 2
( 3 , 3 )
of the basis vector
e 2
( 3 )
≡ 2 Re
(
Q
2 n
Q n
Q n *
Q n *
Q n *
)
is as follows: (13)
N 2
( 3 , 3 )
=
( − 1 )
1 + 1
3 !
(

· 1 ! )
(

· 1 ! )
( − 1 )

3 !
(
1

· 3 ! )
= − 3 .

Another example is the integer coefficient
N 5
( 3 , 3 )
of the basis vector
e 5
( 3 )
≡ 2 Re
(
Q
3 n
Q n *
Q
2 n
*
)
: (14)
N 5
( 3 , 3 )
=
( − 1 )
1
3 !
(
3
1
· 1 ! )
( − 1 )
1 + 1
3 !
(
1
1
· 1 ! )
(
2
1
· 1 ! )
= − 6 .

Each basis vector
e i
( l )
is at the same time a basis vector for all multiparticle correlations whose order is higher than
l
[26]. However, in each multiparticle correlation it enters with a different integer coefficient
N i
( m , l )
. For example in [Eq. (10)], the basis vector
|
Q n
|
2
has the following integer coefficients
1 ,
− 4 ,
and 18, respectively. In order to reveal the integer coefficients
N i
( m , l )
, we divided each observable
∑
i = 1
δ l
N i
( m , l )
e i
( l )
/ m !
by
∑
i = 1
δ l
N i
( l , l )
e i
( l )
/ l !
and obtained the following results shown in Fig. 1 .

The described features helped us to reveal the following binomial-like (Fig. 1) dependence of the
N i
( m , l )
integer coefficients on
m
and
l
: (15)
N i
( m , l )
=
( − 1 )
m + l
( m ! )
2
( m − l )
!
( l ! )
2
N i
( l , l )
.

In practice, one is not always interested in intermediate results of calculation such as the terms written in Eq. (11) but rather the final results. In this case, in the course of building up the code for calculation of Eq. (5) it might be reduced by the same factor
( l ! )
2
, which is present in both equations, Eq. (15) and Eq. (12).

In the case when the multiplicity of an event is equal to the
Q
-cumulant ordering number,
M = m
, the part of the Eq. (5) for which
l = m
, is always equal to
(
M !
)
2
: (16)
∑
l = m
m
(
f
( m , l )
∑
i = 1
δ l
N i
( m , l )
e i
( l )
)
M = m
=
∑
i = 1
δ m
N i
( m , m )
e i
( m )
M = m
=
( M ! )
2
.
This finding, which remained unnoticed so far, is presented in Fig. 2.

Another interesting feature is obtainable by dividing each observable
∑
i = 1
δ m
N i
( m , m )
e i
( m )
by the corresponding
(
m !
)
2
. The obtained results are shown in Fig. 3 for
0 ≤ m ≤ 7
, in addition to the results for
8 ≤ m ≤ 20
after applying the equations Eq. (12) and Eq. (15), which gives further confirmation for the correctness of these two formulas. 10.1103/ycxy-lwqd.f3 3 FIG. 3.
The values of the observables
∑
i = 1
δ m
N i
( m , m )
e i
( m )
/
( m ! )
2
,
m = 1 ,
2 ,
3 , ... ,
20
, for multiplicities
M = 1 ,
2 ,
3 , ... ,
20
. The spline lines are to guide the eye.

Finally, having all the necessary components for calculation of the
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations
〈
2 m
〉
given by Eq. (5), one can compute the weighted average over all events
〈
〈
2 m
〉
〉
(given by Eq. (1) in Ref. [27]). Then one can use the recurrence relation to calculate the
Q
cumulants
c n
{ 2 k }
of any order by knowing all the
Q
cumulants of lower orders [27]: (17)
c n
2 k
=
2 k
−
∑
m = 1
k − 1
k
m
k − 1
m
2 m
c n
2 k − 2 m
.

The
Q
-cumulant-based flow harmonics
v n
{ 2 k }
(
k = 1 ,
2 ,
3 , ...
)
are calculated using the following equation [23] : (18)
v n
2 k
=
a
2 k
− 1
c n
2 k
2 k
,
where coefficients
a
2 k
are obtainable by recursion relation [23]: (19)
a
2 k
= 1 −
∑
m = 1
k − 1
k
m
k − 1
m
a
2 k − 2 m
,
with :
a 2
= 1 ,
which enables easy calculation of high-order
v n
{ 2 k }
.

It has been shown in Ref. [27] that the statistical uncertainties of the
Q
cumulants,
c n
{ 2 k }
, increase slightly, nearly negligible, with the cumulant order due to high correlations between the weighted averages of the multiparticle azimuthally correlations,
〈
〈
2 k
〉
〉
. In addition, the
Q
cumulants of different orders are mutually highly correlated [23,27], which makes their ratios to be potentially high-precision quantities, as will be shown in the next section.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: VALIDATION OF THE METHOD USING A TOY MODEL
sec.p:
In order to validate the above described method, the obtained expressions for the
Q
cumulants up to the 40th order are calculated using azimuthal angles simulated with a toy model. The initial eccentricity
ɛ 2
distribution [28] is simulated using the elliptic power distribution with sets of parameters of different values, depend on the centrality, obtained by Glauber model for 5.02-GeV PbPb collisions [29]. Also, we varied the multiplicity of the events by Gaussian distribution in a range which corresponds to the centrality of a PbPb collision (shown in Table I). The scaling factor
κ 2
between the elliptic flow and the initial eccentricity,
v 2
=
κ 2
ɛ 2
, is chosen to imitate the centrality dependence of the elliptic flow
v 2
measured in Ref. [23]. For each event of a given
v 2
, a simple distribution
1 + 2
v 2
cos
( 2 φ )
is used to generate particle azimuthal angle
φ
. For each centrality,
10
6
events are simulated. The statistical uncertainties are estimated by bootstrapping method which has been proven in Ref. [27] to be in accordance with the analytical method where the proper weighting of the covariance matrix has been applied. 10.1103/ycxy-lwqd.t1 1 TABLE I.
Set of parameters used in the toy-model simulations for each centrality class. Power, α, and ellipticity,
ɛ 0
, are elliptic-power distribution parameters [28]. Mean multiplicity, μ, and standard deviation of the multiplicity, σ, are Gaussian distribution parameters. Scaling factor,
κ 2
, is a linear response between elliptic flow and initial eccentricity. The parameters are chosen to emulate the real charged-particle azimuthal distributions at 5.02-TeV PbPb collisions.
Bin # Centrality class (%) Power parameter (α) Ellipticity parameter (
ɛ 0
) Mean multiplicity (μ) Standard deviation (σ) Scaling factor (
κ 2
)
1 10–15 48.41 0.169 1254 95.62 0.3605
2 15–20 40.62 0.216 1058 85.11 0.3551
3 20–25 32.82 0.261 882.1 75.44 0.3427
4 25–30 26.26 0.296 728.0 66.76 0.3342
5 30–35 22.15 0.329 589.4 59.14 0.3227
6 35–40 17.23 0.349 468.1 51.43 0.3144
7 40–45 13.95 0.367 364.0 44.12 0.3030
8 45–50 11.49 0.385 276.5 37.28 0.2848
9 50–55 9.03 0.400 204.6 30.99 0.2640
10 55–60 6.97 0.402 147.2 25.30 0.2436

As the fluctuations in the initial state are not Gaussian, the
v 2
{ 2 k }
values for
k > 1
will not be the same. This will produce a splitting between different
v 2
{ 2 k }
and they will be ordered as
v 2
{ 2 k }
>
v 2
{
2 (
k +
1
)
}
for any
k > 1
. To make the splitting between the cumulants of different orders explicitly visible, we show in Fig. 4 the relative differences
(
v 2
{ 2 k }
−
v 2
{ 40 }
)
/
v 2
{ 40 }
(
k = 1 , ... , 19
)
as a function of centrality. The magnitudes in Fig. 4 presented with colored markers correspond to different cumulant orders calculated from the simulated data. On the other hand, the corresponding input values, obtained directly by applying the elliptic power distributions, are represented by small black squares connected by straight lines. In Fig. 4 the ordering is clearly seen, as well as the fine splitting between the cumulants of different orders. The relative difference between the cumulants decreases by about one order of magnitude for each increment of the order
k
. An excellent agreement between the black squares and the symbols confirms the correctness of the obtained expressions for the
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations
〈
2 m
〉
. The statistical uncertainties are smaller than the symbol size. In the most central and the most peripheral collisions for the highest cumulant orders, there is a small difference between theoretical values and calculations from the simulated data. However, this difference is within the standard statistical uncertainty limits. 10.1103/ycxy-lwqd.f4 4 FIG. 4.
The relative differences
(
v 2
{ 2 k }
−
v 2
{ 40 }
)
/
v 2
{ 40 }
(
k = 1 ,
2 , ... ,
19
)
as a function of centrality are presented as colored squares. The input values, obtained by directly applying the elliptic power distributions, are represented by small black squares connected by straight lines. Statistical uncertainties are smaller than the symbol size.

sec.label: V.
sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p:
A new method for the fast calculation of any order
Q
-cumulant
v n
{ 2 k }
values is presented in this paper. The method is based on the partition of a non-negative integer
l ≤ m
for calculation of the
2 m
-particle azimuthal correlations. The standard
Q
-cumulants method, in principle, allows calculation of any order multiparticle calculations, but in practice it becomes very difficult to determine the analytical expressions of the cumulants that include azimuthal correlations of six or more particles. The validity of the method has been proven via a toy model that uses the elliptic power distribution to simulate anisotropic emission of particles. To study the fine details of the
v 2
distribution, including higher-order central moments, as well as the hydrodynamic behavior of the QGP, one needs higher-order
Q
-cumulant
v n
{ 2 k }
values. The proposed method enables their calculation.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: The authors acknowledge the support from Ministry of Science, Technological Development and Innovation of Republic of Serbia throughout the theme 0102502, Collaboration Projects JINR-Serbia_P23 and JINR-Serbia_P39, National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 12035006, 12075085, and 12147219) and the U.S. Department of Energy (Grant No. de-sc0012910).

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY
sec.p: No data were created or analyzed in this study.

sec.title:
ROOT code for fast calculation of high-order
Q
cumulants
sec.p: In this code we used an algorithm for fast calculation of integer partitions given in Ref. [30].
